rg_summary: In Jones v. Experian Info. Sols., Inc., 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 267568 (E.D. Mich. Dec. 30, 2025), the court suspected that the pro se plaintiff may have been “led astray” by GenAI while preparing her brief in opposition to defendant’s motion to stay discovery because she included a citation to a case that did not stand for the proposition for which it was cited. Judge Patti reminded the plaintiff that “AI cannot give legal advice” and warned that “even in the absence of bad faith, Rule 11 sanctions for use of AI generated phantom cases may be warranted.”
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Cherleatha B. v. Bisignano, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 266530 (D.S.C. Dec. 29, 2025), the court noted that the pro se plaintiff’s filings “bear characteristics the court has increasingly observed in briefs generated by artificial intelligence (‘AI’) programs.” Taking into consideration the plaintiff’s pro se status and the relative “novelty” of the technology, the court chose not to impose sanctions, but warned the plaintiff that “she could be subject to sanctions . . . should she cite fake, AI-generated legal authority to the court again.” Judge Lydon adopted Magistrate Judge Brown’s Report and Recommendation and dismissed the case without prejudice on other grounds.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Brian NG v. Amguard Ins. Co., 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 266712 (S.D.N.Y. Dec. 29, 2025), the pro se plaintiff cited to a case that “does not appear to exist” to support his “argument as to liability for insurance agents for their individual torts.” Magistrate Judge Stein warned the plaintiff that “[s]ubmitting fake citations to a court is unacceptable” and that future submissions with nonexistent citations may result in sanctions. The court also reminded all parties of Judge Broderick’s recent update to the Individual Rules and Practices in civil cases, which clarified that all parties, regardless of pro se status, must disclose the use of GenAI tools “in the preparation of any documents filed with the Court” and verify the documents’ accuracy, “including all citations.”  Judge Stein granted the defendants’ motion to vacate the clerk’s certificate of default on other grounds.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Requires Disclosure and/or Verification, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Kennedy v. Navy Fed. Credit Union, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 266163 (W.D.N.C. Dec. 29, 2025), the court noted that the plaintiff’s response to the motion to dismiss “clearly violates” the court’s Standing Order on AI (3:24-mc-104), identifying “numerous citations to cases that do not exist or do not arguably stand for the propositions” asserted, and finding that Plaintiff’s “improper use of AI unjustly burdens the Court and the Defendant.” While concluding it could and “certainly should” strike the response for the violation, the court deemed striking unnecessary in light of its dismissal of the complaint and denied remand.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Requires Disclosure and/or Verification, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In LeBer v. Bryan Med. Ctr., No. 8:25CV401, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 264750 (D. Neb. Dec. 23, 2025), the pro se plaintiff “repeatedly cite[d] to a case which appears to be a hallucination of [GenAI], despite [the plaintiff's] certification” that he verified the accuracy of the citations and the court’s previous warning to him about GenAI use. The court again warned the plaintiff to verify the accuracy of the citations and legal authority in his submissions to the court, and that further violations may result in sanctions. The court denied the plaintiff's motion for reconsideration on other grounds.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Allen v. Amazon, Inc., 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 264873 (N.D. Tex. Dec. 23, 2025), the pro se plaintiff included citations to non-existent authority and hallucinated quotes in his motion to compel. As the plaintiff failed to disclose the use of GenAI in his brief, Judge Fitzwater found him to have violated Local Rule 7.2(f)(1) but declined to impose sanctions given his pro se status and first-time offense. However, the court warned that should the plaintiff continue to violate Local Rule 7.2(f)(1), he could face various sanctions, including stricken filings, filing restrictions, fines, and dismissal of the action.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Requires Disclosure and/or Verification, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Fitzgerald v. Brady, 2025 Vt. Super. LEXIS 792 (Vt. Super. Dec. 23, 2025), the pro se plaintiff’s opposition to defendant’s motion to dismiss contained citations to “non-existent authorities and fake quotations,” which court observed may have been created by GenAI. The court warned that any future use of fake or nonexistent legal authorities, whether generated by GenAI or otherwise, violates V.R.C.P. 11 and may result in sanctions. The court also reminded the parties that by presenting a filing, a party certifies that legal contentions are warranted by existing law, noted that fabricated opinions are not “existing law,” and emphasized that self-represented litigants remain bound by Rule 11’s obligations and potential sanctions. On unrelated grounds, the court granted defendant’s motion to dismiss in part and denied it in part.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Donovan v. Clark Cnty. Assessor, 2025 Ind. Tax LEXIS 51 (Ind. Tax Ct. Dec. 22, 2025), the pro se petitioners cited to two non-existent cases in their opening and reply briefs and, during the hearing on the merits, admitted to using an AI tool to assist with drafting. Though the court did not impose any sanctions, the petitioners were “admonishe[d] . . . for failing to confirm the accuracy of their legal presentations” and reminded of their duty to ensure that courts can rely on the accuracy of parties’ assertions.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Smith v. Smith, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 263592 (N.D.N.Y. Dec. 22, 2025), the court found that the plaintiff’s objections and reconsideration briefing bore a “distinctive style and syntax” characteristic of AI and relied on fictitious or mis-cited authorities, attributing the errors to GenAI “hallucinations.” The court adopted the magistrate judge’s report and recommendation, which recommended that (1) the plaintiff's complaint be dismissed with prejudice as to the plaintiff's constitutional claims against certain defendants; (2) the complaint be dismissed without prejudice and with leave to amend as to the plaintiff's constitutional claims against other defendants; and (3) the court decline to exercise its supplemental jurisdiction over the plaintiff's causes of action arising under state law. The court also denied the plaintiff’s motion for reconsideration and warned that future submissions citing fabricated or AI-generated case law may result in sanctions, including striking filings, filing restrictions, monetary penalties, or dismissal.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Charles G. Menk, III v. The Mitre Corp., No. 1:23-cv-00053-JRR (D. Md. Dec. 19, 2025), the court addressed a hallucinated citation included in the plaintiffs’ opposition to a motion to sever. The plaintiffs’ counsel explained that he used ChatGPT for preliminary research, which supplied the nonexistent citation; he provided those materials to co-counsel who drafted the filing, reviewed the response without cite-checking, signed it, accepted responsibility, apologized, and stated he would refrain from using ChatGPT for future legal issues. Citing Rule 11’s reasonable-inquiry requirement and Fourth Circuit guidance on sua sponte sanctions, the court declined to issue a Rule 11 show cause order in light of counsel’s transparency, contrition, and credibility, noting that the public docket entry sufficed as deterrence. The court warned, however, that any future citation to hallucinated authorities or similar conduct will prompt an order to show cause and potential Rule 11 sanctions and/or attorney-misconduct referral, and emphasized that if AI is used, “thorough verification of cited authorities is essential” to avoid rule violations and unnecessary consumption of court resources.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Fantini v. Westrock Co., No. 22-cv-04351, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 262957 (D.N.J. Dec. 19, 2025), the pro se plaintiff cited to “what appear[ed] to be non-existent case law” in his cross-motion for summary judgment. The court warned the plaintiff that pro se parties, like all parties, are bound by Fed. R. Civ. P. 11 and can be sanctioned for “failing to conduct a ‘reasonable inquiry’ into whether ‘the claims, defenses, and other legal contentions are warranted by existing law.’” The court denied the plaintiff’s motion on other grounds.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Disability Rts. Miss. v. Palmer Home for Children, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 263114 (N.D. Miss. Dec. 19, 2025), the plaintiff included various hallucinated citations in three separate motions. In response to the court’s order to show cause, the filing attorney stated that she “relied on an internal reference document developed for efficiency in drafting” and that the “citation errors” were due to “old-fashioned copy and paste” and “paraphrased summary lines for internal use only” that were not removed. The attorney also denied using any GenAI tools but admitted to failing to review certain cases pre-filing. Judge Aycock noted that this is the first instance that a Mississippi federal court considered an attorney’s misconduct with respect to citation mistakes (as opposed to those from a pro se litigant) and was “highly suspicious” that AI was used to generate the citations and did not believe the attorney’s explanations (because, for example, the purported the “summaries” the attorney drafted “misrepresent[ed], either entirely or in part, the facts and rulings of the cases cited”). However, because the attorney did not confirm her use of AI, the court did not conclude that GenAI was the cause of the errors, but noted that it “clearly demonstrate[d] an extreme deviation from [the attorney’s] professional and legal responsibilities.” The court found the attorney to have violated Rule 11 of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure and acted in bad faith by “attempt[ing] to perpetrate fraud on the Court by citing fabricated legal authority in her filings and subsequently making certain misrepresentations.” As the review following the hallucinations was “a complete waste of valuable judicial resources,” Judge Aycock used her inherent powers to impose the following as sanctions: (i) ordering the Clerk of Court to send a copy of this decision to all district and magistrate judges in the district and the State Bar of Mississippi; (ii) ordering the Clerk of Court to file a copy of this decision in the other Northern District of Mississippi case where the plaintiff’s counsel is counsel of record; (iii) requiring plaintiff’s counsel to attend a 3-hour CLE class on AI hallucinations (separate from any CLE obligation under the Mississippi Bar) and submit a copy of her attendance; and (iv) requiring plaintiff’s counsel to pay the defendants $20,883.10 in attorney’s fees and costs within 30 days. The court specifically noted that the plaintiff, a “federally funded nonprofit agency,” was not responsible for the attorney’s misconduct, and further noted that the agency had retained new counsel and “accepted [the offending attorney’s] resignation.”
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting, Court-Imposed Consequences - Attorneys/Law Firms
judge: Judge Sharion Aycock | Judge Sharion Marie Aycock

rg_summary: In Simpson v. Portfolio Recovery Assocs., LLC, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 262777 (W.D. Va. Dec. 19, 2025), the pro se plaintiff included various misrepresented and hallucinated citations in his motion for summary judgment. Although the court was sympathetic to the plaintiff’s “hurdles,” Judge Yoon noted that including false citations wastes resources and could constitute a violation of Rule 11 of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure. Judge Yoon warned the plaintiff that continuing to include hallucinated or inaccurate citations in future filings could result in an order to show cause as to why sanctions under Rule 11 would not be warranted.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Jpmorgan Chase Bank, N.A. v. Buenzli, No. C102530, 2025 Cal. App. Unpub. LEXIS 8160 (Cal. App. 3rd, Dec. 18, 2025), the pro se appellant’s brief contained citations to legal authority that were “at best incorrect, at worse fabricated.” The court ruled that regardless of whether the errors were the result of GenAI, it need not address arguments unsupported by legitimate legal authority. It declined to impose sanctions and affirmed the judgment below.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
judge: Judge Laurie M. Earl | Judge Ronald B. Robie | Judge Shama H. Mesiwala

rg_summary: In Zhang v. National Public Radio, Inc., No. 25-699 (BAH) (D.D.C. Dec. 18, 2025), the court sanctioned plaintiff’s counsel after striking an opposition brief that contained “two completely fictitious and eight seriously mischaracterized citations.” The court recounted counsel’s explanation that he relied on “summaries” from web searches that he was “not even aware” were “being generated by artificial intelligence,” and “failed to read at least 10 cases” cited in the brief. Concluding that the submission “unreasonably” multiplied proceedings and was “singularly egregious,” the court imposed sanctions under 28 U.S.C. § 1927, directing counsel to reimburse the defendant for reasonable attorneys’ fees and costs associated with the stricken filing and the response to the court’s Order to Show Cause. The court also granted the defendant’s motion to dismiss the complaint in full.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting, Court-Imposed Consequences - Attorneys/Law Firms

rg_summary: In Gerou v. George, No. 25-cv-1160-pp, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 261544 (E.D. Wis. Dec. 18, 2025), in an order addressing various motions, the court indicated its suspicion that the pro se petitioners cited GenAI hallucinated cases in their petition demanding proof of origination of funds. The petition contained “multiple direct quotes that do not appear in the cited cases” and citations to “multiple cases that the court could not locate in any legal database.” The court notes that neither “[c]arelessness, good faith, or ignorance,” nor petitioners’ “self-represented status,” excuse them from their obligation under Fed. R. Civ. P. 11 to make a “reasonable inquiry into the supporting law.” Judge Pepper warned petitioners that they may be subject to monetary sanctions if they “file any more documents in any case in this district that contain citations to nonexistent cases or directly quote language that does not appear in a cited case.” The court dismissed the case on other grounds.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Lafayette v. Abrami, 2025 Vt. Super. LEXIS 774 (Vt. Super. Dec. 18, 2025), the court imposed sanctions on the pro se plaintiff for his “repeated use of fake case citations and quotations” in multiple filings, including the initial complaint and preliminary injunction motion. The court further noted that the plaintiff had been warned by at least one other court regarding his use of fake citations. The plaintiff continued to file documents including fabricated cases and quotations after Judge Battles warned that he was considering sanctions. The court found that the plaintiff’s failure to confirm the validity of the cases on which he relied violated V.C.R.P. 11(b)(2). Considering the plaintiff’s history of “serial filings” which violate procedural rules, the plaintiff’s demonstrated bad faith, the burden imposed on the defendant, and the fact that no other sanction would be adequate, the court enjoined the plaintiff from filing any document in the Lamoille Civil Division pro se, unless the plaintiff first obtains permission from the judge.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting, Court-Imposed Consequences - Parties

rg_summary: In Outreach v. Medoff, 2025 Cal. App. Unpub. LEXIS 8133 (Cal. Ct. App. Dec. 17, 2025), the court struck appellant’s reply brief and sanctioned counsel after finding that “the majority of the case citations in the brief were incorrect in some significant way,” emphasizing that attorneys must read cited authorities to ensure they support the stated propositions and noting this duty applies to all court filings, not just appellate briefs. Counsel admitted the reply brief was filed “without [her] review” and that citations “are not correctly used or summarized”; the court rejected her new, inconsistent claim at oral argument that a paralegal filed the wrong version. Citing prior case precedent, the court underscored that filing briefs replete with fabricated or misused authorities—often associated with GenAI misuse—reflects a “‘fundamental abdicat[ion]’” of counsel’s responsibilities. The court imposed $5,070 in monetary sanctions payable to opposing counsel, ordered the opinion forwarded to the State Bar under Bus. & Prof. Code § 6086.7(a)(3), and required counsel to provide the opinion to her client and certify compliance; the underlying judgment was affirmed.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting, Court-Imposed Consequences - Attorneys/Law Firms

rg_summary: In Dodge v. FirstService Residential Ariz. LLC, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 270557 (D. Ariz. Dec. 17, 2025), the plaintiff’s response to the defendant’s motion to dismiss contained “two misrepresented case holdings, five misquoted cases, and one case citation that does not exist.” During the hearing on the court’s order to show cause, plaintiff’s counsel admitted to using Federally Lawyer, an “add-on” to ChatGPT Enterprise that he uses “as a research and drafting tool to assist with his case load.” Given the delay in filing, plaintiff’s counsel stated that he did not independently verify the citations in the response brief and instead asked ChatGPT to perform a cite check using a prompt asking it to confirm that “all legal authority and citation[s] [are] up to date and accurate, meaning is it still good precedent, and are they all real cases?” Judge McNamee ultimately did not impose additional sanctions on the plaintiff’s attorney, the attorney self-reported his conduct to the State Bar of Arizona, and is manually reviewing filings in his other cases to report other errors. The court acknowledged the attorney’s caseload as a solo practitioner and found that the errors ultimately did not prejudice the plaintiff due to merits deficiencies. As such, the “public humiliation in this matter [was] a sufficient remedy.”
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Lowe v. Vilsack, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 261126 (E.D. Cal. Dec. 17, 2025), the court denied Plaintiff’s Rule 30(d)(2) motion for sanctions arising from defense counsel’s conduct during the deposition of the defendant’s National Pandemic Coordinator. In so doing, the court chose to consider the plaintiff’s deposition record that was allegedly created using an AI tool called “Readback Active Reporting” over the defendant’s objection that the process violated Fed. R. Civ. P. 28 and 30. A representative from Readback joined the deposition for its entirety. The defendant described the Readback AI tool as “court reporting reimagined—a remote reporting service powered by patented AI technology and human editors that delivers transcripts at breakneck speeds.” In response, the plaintiff provided evidence from Readback that the company does not use AI “to generate the transcript.” The court concluded that the deposition transcript bore sufficient “indicia of reliability” for the court to consider it for purposes of the sanctions motion, despite any allegation of GenAI use. The court admonished both sides and threatened sanctions for reasons unrelated to GenAI use.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Friend v. Serpa, No. 4D2024-2959, 2025 Fla. App. LEXIS 9580 (Fla. App. 4th DCA, Dec. 17, 2025), in a per curiam affirmance on appeal, a Florida appellate court addressed a pro se appellee’s citation to a “phantom authority” that the court said must be “expressly disregarded by this court.” The three-judge panel reminded appellee that the “court has the authority to sanction” him for using GenAI in a submission without ensuring its accuracy, in violation of Florida procedural rules. The court declined to impose sanctions and affirmed on the merits without further discussion.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
judge: Judge Jeffrey Kuntz | Judge Cory Ciklin | Judge Shannon Shaw
court: Fla. Dist. Ct. App., Fourth Dist.

rg_summary: In Halpern v. FRB of N.Y., No. 3:25-cv-50381, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 261702 (N.D. Ill. Dec. 17, 2025), the pro se plaintiff included a nonexistent quotation and cited a case for a proposition for which it did not stand in his motion response, supplemental memorandum, and motion for clarification. Regardless of “[w]hether this quotation was hallucinated by [GenAI]—which seems likely to be the case—or whether it ‘reflects a widely circulated secondary description” of the underlying case, as the plaintiff claimed, the court warned that the improper use and attribution of the case subjected the plaintiff to the possibility of sanctions. Taking into account the posture of the case and the plaintiff's pro se status, Judge Johnston did not impose sanctions, but “admonish[ed the plaintiff] . . .  that quotations attributed to cases should only come directly from those cases.” The court denied the plaintiff's motion to remand and dismissed the complaint on other grounds without prejudice to the plaintiff filing an amended complaint.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Burlingame v. Argo Priv. Client Grp., Ltd., 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 2161162 (S.D.N.Y. Dec. 17, 2025), in transferring the case against certain defendants to the Northern District of Ohio, Judge Furman opted to “leave it to the transferee court” to address the plaintiff’s misrepresented and hallucinated citations in his opposition to certain defendants’ motion to dismiss. If the case were not transferred, Judge Furman noted that he would have issued an order to show cause as to why plaintiff’s counsel should not be sanctioned for the “well-known problem of ‘hallucinations.’”
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In in re Bryant, 2025 Bankr. LEXIS 3390 (Bankr. M.D.N.C. Dec. 16, 2025), the pro se debtors filed “multiple documents that contain misstatements of law, frivolous arguments, fictitious case cites, and fictitious case holdings,” leading the court to issue a show cause hearing as to why they should not be sanctioned. At the hearing, the court declined to issue sanctions, instead reminding the debtors of their obligations to the court under Federal Rule of Bankruptcy Procedure 9011, which requires unrepresented parties to not make inaccurate or improper filings to the court. When the debtors continued to make arguments that were “frivolous” and unsupported by existing law, including arguments that had previously been rejected by the court, Judge Kahn reminded the debtors that, “fictitious cases and cites are not the only way litigants present frivolous arguments.” Judge Kahn cautioned that “further violations of Rule 9011 will result in sanctions that will include striking pleadings, denying relief requested, and monetary sanctions.” On unrelated grounds, the court denied the debtors’ motion against defendants for sanctions for violating automatic stay and discharge injunction.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Fernandez v. Cnty. of Los Angeles, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 272126 (C.D. Cal. Dec. 16, 2025), the pro se plaintiff submitted a non-existent case citation in a briefing to the court. Upon investigation, both counsel for defendant and the court discovered that the cited case did not exist. The court then admonished plaintiff to read and review the authorities cited in her filings while reminding her that Rule 11 applies equally to unrepresented parties. The court also cited to a case that emphasized that GenAI “hallucinations” can violate Rule 11 and lead to sanctions. On unrelated grounds, the court granted summary judgment for the defendant.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Taylor v. Prince George’s Cnty., 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 259300 (D. Md. Dec. 16, 2025), while recommending denial of the defendants’ motion for attorney’s fees, the court identified multiple citation errors in the plaintiffs’ response brief and expressly noted they are the “types of errors that [GenAI] tools make when hallucinating case citations.”  Although the court stated it was “unclear” whether counsel definitively improperly relied on GenAI and made no recommendation regarding the errors, the magistrate judge flagged the issue for the district court, referencing decisions where courts sanctioned parties and attorneys for reliance on hallucinated, AI-generated case citations.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
judge: Chief Magistrate Judge Timothy J. Sullivan

rg_summary: In Dorsey v. Jones, C.A. No. 2024-0134-CDW (Del. Ch. Dec. 16, 2025), the court ordered that any future filings by defendant and counterclaim plaintiff prepared using GenAI must include a sworn certification confirming GenAI was used, identifying the specific GenAI tool, pinpointing the pages/paragraphs/sections generated by GenAI, and affirming human review for accuracy and completeness, including verification that all legal citations are accurate and support the cited propositions. The order followed the filing of two briefs containing false and mis-cited legal authority and the court’s suspicion that GenAI “hallucination” occurred; it defined GenAI for purposes of the order and warned that failure to comply may result in sanctions.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Requires Disclosure and/or Verification, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
judge: Magistrate in Chancery Christian Douglas Wright

rg_summary: In Holmes Family Tr. v. Multnomah Cty. Assessor, No. TC-MD 250247N, 2025 Or. Tax LEXIS 88 (Or. T.C. Dec. 16, 2025), the court suspected that the plaintiff’s citation to a nonexistent case in its response was the result of GenAI after the defendant directed the court’s attention to a case that “does not exist” and sought sanctions. Magistrate Judge Boomer noted that while the court had the authority to impose sanctions, it declined to do so because it “ha[d] not previously given any direction to Plaintiff concerning the use of AI.” The court instead admonished the plaintiff to verify cases before submission to the court, and that further citations to nonexistent case law may result in sanctions. The court granted in part the defendant’s motion to dismiss the plaintiff’s amended complaint on other grounds.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In in re Zimmer M/L Taper HIP Prosthesis or. M/L Taper HIP Prosthesis with Kinectiv Tech., No. 18-MD-2859 (JMF), 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 266051 (S.D.N.Y. Dec. 16, 2025), the court imposed sanctions on plaintiff’s counsel for filing “papers with AI-generated hallucinations and inaccuracies,” where the plaintiff’s oppositions to defendants’ motion to exclude opinions of a witness and defendants’ motion for summary judgment contained false citations and misrepresentations of case law. The court ordered the parties to confer to agree on the size of the award, which should reflect a reimbursement of the fees and costs the defendants incurred from the problems with the plaintiff’s filings, and to submit a letter to the court if such an agreement could not be reached.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting, Court-Imposed Consequences - Attorneys/Law Firms

rg_summary: In Howell Mgmt. Servs., LLC v. Vashisht-Rota, No. D086055, 2025 Cal. App. Unpub. LEXIS 8006 (Cal. App. 4th Dec. 15, 2025), the court sanctioned a pro se appellant for “prosecuting a frivolous appeal and unreasonably violating the California Rules of Court by citing nonexistent legal authorities.” Judge Irion rejected the plaintiff’s argument that she should not be sanctioned because her GenAI use only resulted in inadvertent, rather than intentional, errors. The court noted that her responsibilities include ensuring the correctness of every citation submitted to the court. The court ordered that the appellant pay $59,235 in attorney fees to the respondent and $15,000 to the clerk of court for her violations and dismissed the appeals on grounds unrelated to the misuse of GenAI.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting, Court-Imposed Consequences - Parties
judge: Judge Joan Irion | Judge Jose Luis Castillo Jr. | Judge Julie Rubin

rg_summary: In Braica v. Frankowski, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 263098 (D. Conn. Dec. 15, 2025), the pro se plaintiff included various unrelated and hallucinated citations in his brief in response to the defendant’s partial motion to dismiss, which the court found to be “highly indicative of unvetted AI use.” The plaintiff attempted to rectify these errors by filing a sur-reply without seeking the court’s leave but maintained that some of his unrelated cases were applicable and continued to cite to hallucinated and irrelevant cases. In response to the court’s order to show cause, the plaintiff admitted to using “publicly available websites” but did not admit to using any GenAI tool. Judge Oliver found the plaintiff’s failure to verify and correct his citations and the fact that he “minimized the role of AI in his filings” to be violative of Rule 11 of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure. Given the “interests of justice and deterrence,” the court struck both the response and sur-reply without leave to amend and warned the plaintiff that “more stringent Rule 11 sanctions” could be imposed if he submits another filing with GenAI hallucinations.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting, Court-Imposed Consequences - Attorneys/Law Firms

rg_summary: In Lexos Media IP, LLC v. Overstock.com, Inc., 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 258649 (D. Kan. Dec. 15, 2025), the court granted in part and denied in part the plaintiff’s motion for leave to file a corrected opposition to a Daubert motion after the defendant identified “nonexistent quotations,” “nonexistent or incorrect citations,” and “misrepresentations about cited authority” in the plaintiff’s original brief—errors the plaintiff attributed to counsel’s unverified use of GenAI. The court held that allowing substantive changes would function as an improper sur-reply and that Plaintiff failed to show excusable neglect under Fed. R. Civ. P. 6(b), emphasizing the prejudice to the defendant and the waste of judicial resources caused by the defective filing. Citing recent authority warning that careless use of GenAI can waste resources and undermine credibility, the court declined to permit a “do-over,” denied any additions of text or authority, and struck the original response. The court ordered Plaintiff to file by December 19, 2025, a corrected brief that omits all nonexistent quotations, nonexistent/incorrect/inapposite citations, and misrepresentations about cited authority, noting that sanctions would be addressed separately.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting, Court-Imposed Consequences - Parties

rg_summary: In Harvey v. Torrent Leasing, Inc., No. 2:25-cv-00824-JAD-DJA, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 260942 (D. Nev. Dec. 15, 2025), the pro se plaintiff’s brief included “several cases” that were “incorrectly cited or [] misquoted.” The court noted that while it does not prohibit litigants from using GenAI, use of “hallucinated or inaccurate legal citations . . .  to support arguments” may violate Fed. R. Civ. P. 11’s requirement that parties’ arguments are “warranted by existing law.” Judge Dorsey warned that additional reliance on “inaccurate or fake authority” may result in sanctions. The court granted the defendant's motion to dismiss on other grounds.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Gardner v. Combs, No. 2:24-cv-07729, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 258523 (D.N.J. Dec. 15, 2025), the plaintiff’s counsel cited to a nonexistent case and “fabricated legal propositions derived from [GenAI] in support of Plaintiff's opposition to Defendants’ motions to dismiss.” Counsel failed to provide an explanation for the false citation after oral argument despite numerous inquiries by defendant’s counsel, leading the court to issue an order to show cause. In response to the order to show cause, counsel admitted that the error was the result of GenAI and “took ‘full responsibility’” for the mistake. The attorney had previously been sanctioned for GenAI use in another jurisdiction, and Judge Gordon noted that despite him taking preventative “corrective actions,” counsel “nevertheless again failed to act with diligence and address issues arising from his reliance on AI research.” The court concluded that his “negligent reliance on unverified (AI-hallucinated) case law and subsequent failure to communicate with respect to the court's follow-up inquiry” violated Fed. R. Civ. P. 11. The court imposed a $6,000 sanction on the attorney, describing the penalty as “on the higher end of the range due to his repeated behavior. The court also ordered that counsel self-report the opinion and order to show cause to NJ and NY attorney licensing entities and serve copies on “his client and discuss their implications with her.”
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Applies to AI Used for Research, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting, Court-Imposed Consequences - Attorneys/Law Firms

rg_summary: In Maturin v. T-Mobile USA, Inc., 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 258578 (D.N.M. Dec. 15, 2025), the court recommended granting in part and denying in part defendant’s motion for sanctions alleging bad-faith conduct and frivolous pleadings, ordering the plaintiff to pay defendant’s reasonable expenses for investigating “false citations” used in the plaintiff’s filings and for litigating the sanctions motion and supplement. The court further recommended that, in all future filings, the plaintiff must disclose any use of AI in drafting and certify under Rule 11 that all AI-provided information, including all case citations, has been verified for accuracy. While denying terminating sanctions, the court warned that failure to comply with court orders or sanctions may result in dismissal, and permitted defendant to seek additional sanctions for noncompliance.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Requires Disclosure and/or Verification, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting, Court-Imposed Consequences - Parties

rg_summary: In Jeter v. Adusa Transp. LLC, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 270555 (D.S.C. December 15, 2025), the pro se plaintiff’s response to defendant’s motion to dismiss cited nonexistent authorities, misquoted existing case law, and invoked cases for propositions they do not support—conduct the court observed is “consistent with on the use of [GenAI] . . . . without proper verification.” The court cautioned that misusing GenAI in this manner “may violate Rule 11” and emphasized that misquotations and nonexistent authorities reflect failing to read governing law. Given the novelty of GenAI and the plaintiff’s pro se status, the court declined sanctions but instructed that any GenAI-assisted submissions must be verified by reading the actual cases, rules, and statutes. The court warned that continued failure to verify citations and quotations will wrongfully multiply proceedings and may evidence bad faith, exposing the plaintiff to sanctions in the future. The court recommended granting in part and denying in part the motion to dismiss on other grounds.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Williams v. Assistant DA John R. Canavan, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 258853 (M.D. Pa. Dec. 15, 2025), the court addressed the pro se plaintiff’s reliance on GenAI in multiple filings, noting an “uptick” in pro se litigants using “cut-and-paste AI outputs” and finding that the plaintiff’s guardian-ad-litem motion included an AI “hallucination.” The court also observed AI-aided objections that included a table of authorities mismatched to the filing and stylistic discrepancies from Plaintiff’s handwritten IFP motion. Citing Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 11(b)(2)-(c), the court warned the plaintiff that future “unchecked reliance on AI . . . will result in sanctions,” including striking pleadings and dismissal with prejudice. Substantively, the court dismissed the pro se plaintiff’s claims without prejudice on other grounds.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In N.Z. v. Fenix Int’l Ltd., 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 259988 (C.D. Cal. Dec. 12, 2025), the court denied the plaintiffs’ motion for leave to withdraw multiple opposition briefs and file “corrective” briefs after identifying AI-generated inaccuracies, and imposed Rule 11 sanctions on the plaintiff’s counsel and co-counsel for misuse of GenAI and failure to verify AI-modified content. The court found that four opposition briefs contained hallucinated authorities, mischaracterized law and quotations, and incorrect record citations—including misstatements of the court’s prior order—and held that allowing corrective briefs would “not remediate, but rather exacerbate” prejudice where defendants had already expended substantial resources addressing the tainted filings and where proposed corrections still contained errors. The plaintiff’s counsel also admitted to using ChatGPT to “draft large portions of the briefs, analyze the legal research [the attorney] performed,” and “combine and shorten” materials. The court also observed that the attorney failed to realize when ChatGPT modified text and authorities, did not verify accuracy, and did not inform her co-counsel of her GenAI use; and co-counsel, relying on the drafting attorney’s prior work and facing a deadline, did not perform a full cite check. Concluding that counsel had certified content not warranted by existing law and failed to supervise consistent with Cal. R. Prof’l Conduct 5.1 and the court’s AI standing order, the court sanctioned both the primary offending individual attorney and his law firm (jointly and severally) $10,000 and sanctioned the plaintiffs’ individual co-counsel $3,000, required status reporting on remedial measures and certification that all future citations accurately support the asserted propositions, directed service of the order on the California and Arizona state bars and each individual plaintiff, and discharged the order to show cause.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting, Court-Imposed Consequences - Attorneys/Law Firms

rg_summary: In Couvrette v. Wisnovsky, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 273533 (D. Or. Dec. 12, 2025), the court imposed sanctions on the plaintiffs and the plaintiffs’ attorney for filing three briefs containing fabricated cases and quotations, which the court held constituted violations of Fed. R. Civ. P. 11 and local rules. The plaintiffs continued to cite to nonexistent cases after the defendants called the errors to their attention and suggested GenAI misuse. In response to the defendants’ motion for sanctions, the plaintiffs simply deleted the non-existent citations and quotation marks while leaving the fabricated legal contentions unchanged. Plaintiffs argued that “[t]he inclusion of inaccurate citations was inadvertent and the result of reliance on an automated legal citation tool” and that they had taken proper corrective action. Defendants then re-filed their motion for sanctions. At the sanctions hearing, the court found that the plaintiffs failed to take corrective action and instead “attempted a coverup,” violating Fed. R. Civ. P. 11 and the Oregon Rules of Professional Conduct. The court also found the plaintiff himself was likely the person using GenAI to draft and “share[d] responsibility [with his counsel] for the use of non-existent cases and fabricated quotations,” thus warranting sanctions against the plaintiff as well. As sanction, the court struck without leave to refile plaintiffs’ summary judgment briefing; imposed a $15,500 fine plus attorney’s fees and expenses on the plaintiff’s counsel; vacated the order allowing former local counsel to withdraw and ordered him show cause why he should not be subject to sanctions; and dismissed the plaintiff’s claims with prejudice.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting, Court-Imposed Consequences - Attorneys/Law Firms, Court-Imposed Consequences - Parties

rg_summary: In Matthew NG v. Amguard Ins. Co., 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 260114 (S.D.N.Y. Dec. 12, 2025), the court noted an inaccurate citation in the plaintiff’s letter motion that “provides no support” for the proposition asserted and assumed the filing “may have been drafted with the assistance of a [GenAI] tool.” The court expressly directed compliance with Judge Broderick’s Individual Rule 4(J) (which requires any party who uses GenAI in preparing a filing to disclose that use and to certify independent review and accuracy verification of all AI-drafted portions). While denying the plaintiff’s requests on service and a stay, the court set a deadline for the plaintiff to file either an opposition to the motion to dismiss or an amended complaint and emphasized future adherence to the court’s AI disclosure and verification rule.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Requires Disclosure and/or Verification, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: Judge McGee’s Revised Administrative Order on Artificial Intelligence for Judicial District 25 supersedes the court’s prior July 23, 2024 order, applies immediately to all civil and criminal proceedings in Cabarrus County, and lays out requirements for AI use and disclosure, concerning both court filings and evidentiary issues.  While the order does not bar the use of GenAI for litigation purposes, it reminds litigants that each submission to the court is the ultimate responsibility of the signing attorney or party, and requires  "[h]uman-in-the-Loop (HITL) oversight” (i.e, having a human decision-maker “independently review, verify, and approve any AI output before it is relied on, acted on, or submitted in any court proceeding”). The order also reminds attorneys of their professional duties to “maintain technical competence regarding” the technologies used and to ensure they do not place confidential or privileged information into public AI tools. With respect to recording and transcription, the order provides that only official reporters or clerks may use AI tools to capture, record, transcribe, process, or alter audio or video without judicial authorization. Jurors are prohibited from using GenAI for any purpose, while litigants may use GenAI for non-disruptive legal research during hearings with appropriate attorney oversight. Finally, the order requires that parties must disclose no later than 90 days before trial if they have used GenAI to “generate[], offer[], materially alter[], enhance[] or analyze[]” evidence. After that window, disclosure must be immediate. By contrast, non-substantive AI use (such as “word processing, spell-check, grammar or “administrative tasks,” GenAI use in discovery, and use of GenAI to aid court reporters or transcriptionists) does not require disclosure. When there is reasonable basis for doubt as to the accuracy of a document, parties must use “best efforts” to verify the evidence’s validity and must meet and confer to resolve concerns about AI-generated evidence (and such concerns must be raised before trial absent extraordinary circumstances). Should parties fail to comply with this order, the court may impose sanctions, but the court will favorably consider “timely, good-faith disclosures” and taking “reasonable steps to verify authenticity and reliability.”
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting
judge: Senior Resident Superior Court Judge Martin B. McGee

rg_summary: Effective December 8, 2025, the Pennsylvania Supreme Court’s Interim Policy on the Use of Generative Artificial Intelligence by Judicial Officers and Court Personnel governs court personnel’s use of GenAI tools and provides insight for parties on how to approach the use of GenAI. The policies define GenAI as an all-encompassing term for “algorithms and/or computer processes that use artificial intelligence to generate text, audio, or images based on user prompts.” Before any GenAI tool is used, it must first be approved by court leadership who ensure that the tool will keep information “confidential and privileged.” Court personnel must, however, presume that GenAI inputs will not be treated as confidential and privileged and remain aware of GenAI’s “capabilities and limitations,” including hallucinations, biases, and inaccuracies. The court welcomes GenAI use by its personnel for, among other things, document summaries, preliminary legal research, and drafts and edits of their own work. Ultimately, the GenAI user remains responsible for the output’s completeness and accuracy.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Applies to AI Used for Research

rg_summary: In Mullins v. Duquesne Univ. of the Holy Spirit, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 251137 (W.D. Pa. Dec. 5, 2025), the court granted the defendant’s motion to strike the pro se plaintiff’s reply brief where the plaintiff misused of GenAI by including multiple citation and quotation errors “that appear to be the result of Plaintiff using [GenAI] drafting tools,” including a “phantom quote.” The plaintiff “implict[ly]” admitted to using GenAI tools, which resulted in hallucinations, but the court criticized his “unwillingness to affirmatively admit” to such use. Emphasizing that neither the court nor opposing counsel is responsible for “fact‑checking” a party’s filings, the court held that filing AI‑generated content without thorough pre‑filing verification “wastes” judicial and party resources. The court therefore: (1) struck the reply but granted leave to refile a corrected version; and (2) ordered that all future filings must include a certification disclosing whether “he has utilized any [AI] drafting tools in the research and/or drafting of the document being filed,” identifying each AI tool, pinpointing the specific AI‑generated portions, and certifying the accuracy of all citations and authorities, warning that violations may trigger sanctions under Rule 11 or other authority.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Requires Disclosure and/or Verification, Applies to AI Used for Research, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Gibbs v. City of Compton, 2025 Cal. Super. LEXIS 73275 (Cal. Super. Dec. 4, 2025), the court found the plaintiff's opposition to the defendant’s motion to set aside default judgment “unreliable” on its face and that the plaintiff’s counsel drafted it using GenAI without properly reviewing it for accuracy. The court reached these conclusions because the plaintiff's filings (including a declaration by the plaintiff’s counsel) incorrectly asserted that defendant's memorandum violated the page limit, misquoted a court form, and included a nonexistent case quotation. The court also found GenAI output language at the end of the plaintiff’s opposition briefing that clearly demonstrated that “Plaintiff’s counsel utilized an AI tool to draft and submit the papers.” Judge Wilson therefore “disregard[ed] improper citations and arguments lacking proper legal citation” and “exercise[d] [the court’s] discretion to afford the declaration accompanying Plaintiff's Opposition less credence,” declining to consider the plaintiff’s arguments because of lack of legal support. The court ultimately granted the defendant’s motion to set aside the entry of default and default judgment.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting, Court-Imposed Consequences - Attorneys/Law Firms, Court-Imposed Consequences - Parties

rg_summary: In Dorsey v. Ponce, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 250368 (N.D. Ill. Dec. 4, 2025), the court expressed concerns about generative AI-fabricated citations in the pro se plaintiff’s briefing. After discovering that several cited cases “do not exist,” the court expressed its suspicion that “many of these cases are the product of an artificial intelligence tool,” and ordered that all future filings must include a traditional reporter or database citation for any case cited. The court warned that any future “fabricated or hallucinated” citations will result in sanctions, including striking the filing and potential dismissal of the action. The underlying motion to dismiss was granted in part and denied in part on other grounds.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Hill v. Okla. Cnty. Crim. Just. Auth., 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 250359 (W.D. Okla. Dec. 4, 2025), the court adopted a magistrate judge’s recommendation imposing sanctions on plaintiff’s counsel for citing non‑existent cases and using ChatGPT‑generated content in briefing, finding the conduct part of a broader pattern of missed deadlines, erroneous filings, and a “general lack of care.” At a show‑cause hearing, counsel “confirmed that he used Chat GPT” to draft a response and attempted to deflect responsibility to staff, which the magistrate judge deemed inconsistent with Rule 11 obligations and harmful to the integrity of the judicial process. The court required plaintiff’s counsel to pay attorney’s fees and costs associated with the show-cause hearing; disclose in all future pleadings whether GenAI was used, identify the specific tool was used, and certify that any AI‑drafted portions (including all citations and legal authorities) have been checked for accuracy; and complete (within 90 days) at least three hours of CLE in ethics or legal technology, including at least one hour on the use of GenAI in the legal context.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting, Court-Imposed Consequences - Attorneys/Law Firms

rg_summary: In Moore v. City of Del. City, 2025 U.S. App. LEXIS 31411 (10th Cir. Dec. 3, 2025), the court dismissed the pro se appellant’s case for citing eleven nonexistent cases and misrepresenting the holdings of at least two cases in her opening brief. The court “suspected” GenAI use and ordered the appellant to provide copies of the nonexistent cases or explain how the errors occurred in the brief, identify which GenAI tool she used in preparing the document, and show cause why she should not be sanctioned. The court was not convinced by the appellant’s response to the show cause order (and indeed found that she also “turned to a GenAI tool for assistance in drafting her response”), and concluded that the appellant’s “citation of nonexistent cases and her misrepresentation of at least one other case has impaired judicial efficiency and amounts to a failure to comply with Fed. R. App. P. 28(a)(8)(A)’s requirement that an appellant's brief cite supporting authority.” The court dismissed the appeal and ordered that the appellant disclose GenAI use and verify the accuracy of her citations in future filings. Finally, the court warned “future litigants, pro se and those represented by counsel alike, that outright dismissal—without a merits ruling—may be an appropriate sanction in a given case,” as may monetary or other sanctions (including awarding the opposing party’s attorney fees incurred from the “unscrutinized use of GenAI”).
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Requires Disclosure and/or Verification, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting, Court-Imposed Consequences - Parties
judge: Timothy M. Tymkovich | Bobby R. Baldock | Gregory A. Phillips | Judge Timothy M. Tymkovich | Judge Bobby R. Baldock | Judge Gregory A. Phillips

rg_summary: Judge Wang’s standing order requires that every filing include a signed certification addressing “the use, or non-use, of generative AI in preparing the filing” and affirming that any portions of the filing “generated using AI” have been independently reviewed for accuracy and contain no fictitious cases or authorities. The certification must be signed by all contributors to the filing.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Requires Disclosure and/or Verification, Applies to AI Used for Research, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Villalovos-Gutierrez v. Van de Pol, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 253583 (E.D. Cal. Dec. 3, 2025), the court expressly ordered that counsel for defendant “shall not file or otherwise present to the court any documents which contain AI-hallucinated citations or fictitious or non-existent legal citations,” warning that failing to independently confirm the existence, accuracy, and veracity of any AI-generated or indirectly sourced citation is grounds for sanctions. The order noted several examples where defendants’ citations either appeared fictitious or did not support the propositions asserted, highlighting counsel’s duty to verify authorities before filing. The court granted the plaintiffs’ motion to compel discovery on other grounds.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In United States v. Boehm, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 235285, the pro se defendant included inaccurate legal arguments and quotations “that appear to be generated by [GenAI] using legitimate case citations” in his request for an order clarifying the conditions of his supervised release. Although noting that the “improper use of [GenAI] wastes the time and resources of the court,” Judge Munley took into account the defendant’s status as a “formerly incarcerated individual attempting to advocate his position as he transitions from a prison setting to society” and many GenAI tools’ “tout[ing] [of] their ability to conduct in-depth research,” and therefore declined to award sanctions. The court admonished the defendant for the misrepresentations, however, and dismissed the motion without prejudice. Judge Munley further ordered that for any future filings, the defendant must file a separate affidavit disclosing the details of any GenAI use, and certifying that he has personally checked the accuracy of each citation.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Requires Disclosure and/or Verification, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Charles Ringo v. Colquhoun Design Studio, LLC, 2025 Ore. App. LEXIS 1957 (Or. Ct. App. Dec. 3, 2025), the court directed respondents’ counsel to pay $2,000 for submitting a brief “littered with fabricated cases, a fabricated quotation, and fabricated substantive law.” Judge Ashby noted that the fabricated cases “likely” resulted from GenAI use and constitute a violation of ORCP 17C(3)’s certification that “the claims, defenses, and other legal positions taken in the pleading, motion or other document are warranted by existing law or by a nonfrivolous argument for the extension, modification or reversal of existing law or the establishment of new law,” as well as a violation of the attorney’s professional duties. The court described how the attorney’s conduct “strain[ed] [the court’s] limited judicial resources” and “jeopardizes the rule of law,” as “[t]he injection of false precedent into the practice of law shakes the foundation of our judicial system.” Judge Ashby thus ordered monetary sanctions of $500 for each false citation and $1,000 for each false quotation or statement of law, totaling to $2,000. The court allowed counsel to resubmit the stricken brief, but required that all submissions by this counsel going forward “contain a certification that (1) counsel drafted the brief and did not use [GenAI] to produce a draft of the brief; (2) counsel has read each case and each other source of law cited in the brief; and (3) counsel has verified that every source of law cited, quoted[,] or paraphrased exists.”
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Requires Disclosure and/or Verification, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting, Court-Imposed Consequences - Attorneys/Law Firms

rg_summary: In Bonsignore v. N.Y. Dep’t. Tax'n & Fin., No. 25-CV-6324 (S.D.N.Y. Dec. 3, 2025), Judge Broderick notified the parties of Rule 4(J) of his Individual Rules and Practices (which requires disclosure of GenAI use in the “preparation” of any filings and a “certification” of independent review and accuracy verification). The court instructed the parties to submit a short declaration identifying which documents submitted to the court, if any, they prepared using GenAI since the rule came into effect only recently (on October 29, 2025). The court directed any party using GenAI to sign and submit the review and accuracy verification certificate or the filing will be stricken.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Requires Disclosure and/or Verification, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Delta Western LLC v. May, No. 24-2-09298-6 (Wa. Super. Dec. 3, 2025), the court ordered the defendant’s counsel to show cause for why he should not be sanctioned for including false quotations and “inaccurate and misleading characterizations” of two cases in his briefing on the petition for fees. In response, counsel apologized and admitted that he had used GenAI in his research and failed to adequately check his quotations and citations. (ECF 156).  Accordingly, the court sanctioned the defendant’s counsel $1,000 and required him to attend at least three hours of CLE courses about AI issues in the legal profession.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting, Court-Imposed Consequences - Attorneys/Law Firms

rg_summary: In Magee v. New Balance Athletics., Inc., 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 234410 (E.D. Ark. Dec. 2, 2025), the pro se plaintiff used GenAI to prepare his response to the defendant's motion to dismiss, in which he allegedly “misstated the holdings of twenty-seven of the thirty cases cited, invented false quotations from twenty-three cases, and fabricated two cases.” In response, the plaintiff apologized and promised to do better in the future. Judge Marshall “accept[ed] [the plaintiff's] apology” but “admonish[ed] him,” warning that he must verify his sources in future filings and show cause why the court should not impose a monetary sanction for his “generative-AI-infected response.” The court ultimately granted the defendant’s motion to strike the plaintiff's response and denied pending motions to dismiss and for summary judgment.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Jarrus v. Governor of Michigan, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 234830, the pro se plaintiffs objected to the Magistrate Judge’s order for the plaintiffs to show cause for the use of GenAI in drafting their motion to amend their complaint. Judge Behm suspected that the plaintiffs again used GenAI in their objection. The court noted that the plaintiffs’ briefing appeared to be “nothing more than a copy-paste from a chatbot-style generative AI tool” with a “series of . . . false citations” of real cases with explanations that did not match the real case. Judge Behm noted that the use of GenAI for drafting without independent citation checking “unquestionably” violates Fed. R. Civ. P. 11. After the Magistrate Judge's warning (see Jarrus v. Governor of Michigan, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 221984 (E.D. Mich. Nov. 7, 2025)), the court ordered plaintiffs to show cause why they should not be sanctioned. Judge Behm highlighted that Rule 11 “imposes obligations on each and every signatory to a briefing, not just the person who was the primary drafter.” Thus, even though one of the plaintiffs did not draft the documents and only reviewed them at a “very general level” before signing, the court held her “equally responsible for the use of misrepresented or otherwise improper citations,” and warned that further misconduct could result in “a dismissal of her claims in their entirety.” Although the plaintiffs admitted to misusing GenAI (ChatGPT specifically) in their filings, the court found that sanctions were warranted. Judge Behmt adopted Magistrate Judge Patti's recommendation of $200 per GenAI citation and imposed a $600 fine ($300 per plaintiff), warning that failure to pay may result in dismissal. The court reasoned that one of the plaintiff's $20 per month spending on a ChatGPT subscription suggested an ability to pay sanctions. The court further warned that it will consider dismissal of the case if the plaintiffs file further briefings “with even a single misrepresented, misquoted, or fictitious case that is caused by the use of [GenAI].”
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting, Court-Imposed Consequences - Parties

rg_summary: In Ford v. James Koutoulas & Lgbcoin, Ltd., 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 234696 (M.D. Fla. Dec. 2, 2025), the defendants' motion for summary judgment contained several citations that the court and the plaintiffs suspected were GenAI hallucinations, where the court was “unable to locate” the cited authorities. The defendants did not deny plaintiffs’ allegations that their filings contained AI hallucinations. Judge Byron noted that citing to nonexistent cases is a “misrepresentation to the Court,” is sanctionable under Fed. R. Civ. P. 11, and violates the Florida local rules and rules of professional conduct. The court warned that continued “unethical practices” may result in sanctions. The court denied the defendants' motion on other grounds.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Johnson v. Insight Glob., LLC, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 253973 (N.D. Ga. Dec. 2, 2025), the court admonished the pro se plaintiff for submitting a motion to remand that contained “citations to non-existent caselaw, which may [have been] the result of [GenAI].” The court warned the plaintiff that his “pro se status does not shield him from the same duty of candor to the court as imposed on any other litigant,” and that further reliance on non-existent case law may result in sanctions. The court dismissed the motion on other grounds.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Button v. McCawley, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 234737 (S.D. Fla. Dec. 2, 2025), the pro se plaintiffs cited numerous GenAI-hallucinated cases in their response to the defendant's motion for attorney's fees. Magistrate Judge Augustin-Birch noted that the plaintiffs “falsely attribute[d] quotes” and “cite[d] non-existent cases.” The court ultimately decided not to recommend imposing sanctions because of the plaintiffs’ pro se status and the fact that it was their first infraction. However, the court warned that “going forward, Plaintiffs must ensure that all of their caselaw quotations and citations are accurate.” Magistrate Judge Augustin-Birch recommended granting the motion in part and denying it in part on other grounds.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Shayan v. Shakib, 2025 LEXIS 595242 (Cal. Ct. App. 2d. Dec. 1, 2025), the appellant's attorney filed a brief containing numerous fabricated case citations and a transcript from a hearing in a different matter, and the respondent asked the court to strike the brief and dismiss the appeal as a sanction. In response, the appellant’s attorney claimed that the errors were the result of his staff failing to replace placeholder language from the draft brief, not the result of GenAI use, that he checked each citation, and that the errors reflected only “minor” wording differences. The court disagreed with that the attorney’s characterization and noted that both the brief and the opposition contained “inaccurate representations of case holdings.” The court held that these errors violated local rules, the Ca. Code Civ. Proc., and the rules of professional conduct, regardless of whether the fabrications were the result of GenAI use. Furthermore, Judge Fahey observed that even if the attorney did review the accuracy of the citations, “the rules of this court require him to present accurate, truthful representations of the facts and law to this court,” which the attorney “did not do.” The court ordered that the attorney pay $7,500 in sanctions to the clerk of court (due to the time the court spent verifying the citations, the attorney's failure to accept responsibility, and the continued mistakes in the opposition motion) and also struck the appellant’s opening brief and required the filing of a corrected brief within 10 days. Judge Fahey served a copy of the order on the State Bar.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting, Court-Imposed Consequences - Attorneys/Law Firms

rg_summary: In her guidance document entitled “Guidance on Responsible AI Use in Court Proceedings,” Magistrate Judge Braswell provides a framework for parties practicing before her court to address AI-related issues, particularly hallucinations in court filings. Notably, the guidance “is not an order and does not establish new rules or requirements” and “should [not] be interpreted to modify or supersede any applicable law, rule, professional obligation, or order issued by another judge.” The guidance acknowledges that AI “presents opportunities for innovation, growth, and increased access to justice” and encourages parties to “learn to leverage it responsibly,” while also noting the various “challenge[s]” that AI poses, only “one part” of which is hallucinations. The court’s guidance emphasizes that “all inaccurate statements made to a tribunal, regardless of how they were generated, are a serious matter,” and that the “same rules apply” regardless of the source of the error. When hallucinations are found, the guidance encourages parties to confer to explore remedial measures, inform the court, and escalate only if the drafter refuses to engage in good faith. The guidance lists a variety of factors the court will consider when evaluating submission of AI hallucinations, including whether the drafter engaged in good faith, took responsibility, took immediate steps to mitigate harm, follows an AI Use Policy, took reasonable steps to verify accuracy before filing, and whether the drafter is a pro se party.  The court will also consider the nature and extent of the hallucinations, whether they caused meaningful prejudice, and whether they resulted in a significant waste of judicial resources. Finally, the guidance encourages caution in the legal industry regarding AI-created bias, ensuring adequate data privacy safeguards, and ensuring human autonomy over important decisions.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: Effective December 1, 2025, Judge Mazzant’s General Order 25-07 (E.D. Tex. Oct. 31, 2025) amends the Eastern District of Texas Local Rule CV-11(g) on GenAI use. Prior to the amendment, the rule warned only pro se litigants that they “remain responsible for the accuracy and quality” of their submissions to the court and cautioned that GenAI “may produce factually or legally inaccurate content.” As revised, all litigants are now “bound by the requirements of Fed. R. Civ. P. 11” and must “review and verify all content” (rather than just “computer-generated content,” as the Rule previously required) when using GenAI. Judge Mazzant’s Comment indicated that the amendments were prompted by an “increasing number of filings” where litigants “fail[ed] to review and verify the factual and legal accuracy” of content produced by GenAI. The parallel rule for attorneys, Local Rule AT-3(m), was also amended to conform to LR CV-11(g).
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Bey v. Glass, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 233422 (M.D. Fla. Dec. 1, 2025), the pro se plaintiff cited a nonexistent case in his complaint. Judge Jung suspected that the hallucinated citation was the result of GenAI use in drafting after the court “cannot find . . . based on independent research” a case the plaintiff cited and warned the plaintiff that the court has the “inherent authority” under Fed. R. Civ. P. 11 to impose sanctions for GenAI use, “including the complete dismissal and termination of the case.” The court dismissed the plaintiff's complaint without prejudice on other grounds.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Hanson v. Nest Home Lending, LLC, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 233214 (D. Colo. Nov. 28, 2025), the court made absolute its prior order to show cause after finding that the pro se plaintiffs’ complaint, motions, and responses contained “fabricated legal authority” and “hallucinated citations” in violation of Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 11. The plaintiffs argued they acted in good faith and that any errors stemmed from their “ignorance of the law,” but the court held that Rule 11’s objective‑reasonableness standard applies to all filers, including pro se litigants, and that good faith does not excuse submission of fake or inaccurate citations. The court emphasized the harms caused by such misuse, including wasted party and judicial resources, diversion from merits issues, and reputational harm to the legal system, and noted that plaintiffs had been on notice of problems in their complaint yet continued filing papers with fictitious or mischaracterized authority. The court further observed that plaintiffs’ response compounded the issue by misquoting precedent and citing out‑of‑jurisdiction law for irrelevant propositions. Magistrate Judge Starnella recommended sweeping non‑monetary remedies tailored to deterrence and docket integrity, including striking the tainted filings identified in the show‑cause order, granting the plaintiffs 45 days to file an amended complaint, and requiring that all future filings include a detailed certification, in table format, listing each citation, confirming its existence and that it supports the proposition asserted, and identifying the source where the authority was obtained. The court also directed plaintiffs to use “legitimate” research databases and to contact the Federal Pro Se Clinic regarding publicly available research resources. The court denied pending motions to dismiss as moot in light of its recommendation and warned that continued misconduct could lead to dismissal. While recognizing the increasing use of GenAI, the court underscored that parties must independently verify accuracy and that “using a fake opinion to support an argument is a violation of Rule 11(b)(2),” regardless of intent.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Applies to AI Used for Research, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting, Court-Imposed Consequences - Parties

rg_summary: In Anaya v. Target Corp., No. 2:25-cv-09135-SB-RAO (Nov. 26, 2025), the court sanctioned plaintiff's counsel in the amount of $1,250 for filing a brief “containing a case that did not stand for the cited proposition” in support of the plaintiff's motion to remand. Judge Blumenfeld noted that the court had previously cautioned the plaintiff about the importance of abiding by court rules, warning that further violations could result in sanctions. In response to the order to show cause, the attorney explained that his associate used “AI to refine a small portion of the Reply" but he did not disclose the use because he did not realize the associate had used GenAI at the time. Judge Blumenfeld determined that this conduct violated Fed. R. Civ. P. 11. The court further “admonished” the firm's managing partner based on his “indirect responsibility” for “failing to take appropriate steps to ensure firmwide compliance with all applicable rules and orders.” Judge Blumenfeld determined that the despite his candor, the filing attorney’s “use of AI without proper disclosure and blatant misuse of case law in reliance on AI—coupled with his careless oversight of the case as lead counsel for Plaintiff—warrant appropriate sanctions.” The court ordered $1,250 in monetary sanctions and notification of the sanction to the State Bar of California.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting, Court-Imposed Consequences - Attorneys/Law Firms

rg_summary: In Doc App, Inc. v. Leafwell, Inc., 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 232188 (M.D. Fla. Nov. 26, 2025), the court granted the defendant’s motion for sanctions after the plaintiff’s counsel included various inaccurate citations (including to nonexistent and out-of-context cases) in its motion for a temporary restraining order and preliminary injunction. The court found that the plaintiff’s attorney violated Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 11(b) for including erroneous citations due to his “reckless use of AI.” The court concluded that sanctions were particularly warranted where counsel responded to the court’s order to show cause in a “colossal [] collection of excuses and projection” with a “defiant tone and condescending communications with and about his opposing counsel,” and where plaintiff’s counsel was not forthcoming about his use of AI. Judge Chappell held that the attorney failed to take responsibility for his actions and reminded him of his role as “an officer of the court” with “a duty of candor to the tribunal.” As sanctions, Judge Chappell ordered plaintiff’s counsel to: (i) pay the defendant’s attorneys’ fees and costs associated with their response to the motion; (ii) file a copy of this order to any complaint filed in the next 2 years in the Middle District of Florida; (iii) attend a Florida CLE class on legal ethics and AI by January 30, 2026 and file a certificate of completion by February 20, 2026; and (iv) send a copy of this order to his client’s CEO and file an affidavit confirming that the CEO has read the order. The court also referred plaintiff’s counsel to the Florida Bar for “appropriate discipline” and asked the clerk to send the entire case record to the Florida Bar.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting, Court-Imposed Consequences - Attorneys/Law Firms

rg_summary: In Peak v. Maraj-Petty, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 232924 (S.D.N.Y. Nov. 26, 2025), in an order continuing the stay of the case, Judge Broderick raised his concern that the pro se plaintiff was using GenAI throughout the litigation “because of his persistent misquotes and interpretation of cases.” Most recently, the plaintiff included two hallucinated quotations in an opposition letter. Though the court was sympathetic to the plaintiff’s status of representation, the plaintiff was reminded of Individual Rule of Practice in Civil Cases 4(J) that requires parties to disclose any use of AI and certify that all GenAI-drafted portions are verified and accurate.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Requires Disclosure and/or Verification, Suggests Cautious Use of AI, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting

rg_summary: In Andre v. Warden, FCI Danbury, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 232986 (D. Conn. Nov. 25, 2025), the pro se petitioner filed a motion for relief from judgment that, when considered in light of other pleadings, appeared “unusually well-written and formatted for a pro se litigant” and prompted the court to think they were drafted by an attorney. Upon further review, the court discovered that the motion contained “multiple significant hallucinations” and inaccurate citations. During the hearing on the court’s order to show cause, the petitioner admitted that he heavily relied on AI tools during the drafting process and even asked some of his “friends” to “ask AI certain questions” and then have “AI ‘draft up motions.’” Judge Oliver found that the petitioner acted in “subjective bad faith,” thereby violating Rule 11 of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure, because he made “misrepresentations to the Court as to the origins of the filings” by directing “third parties to use generative AI and then concealed his actions from the Court when given the opportunity to accept responsibility.” As sanctions, the court ultimately struck the petitioner’s motion for relief with prejudice.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting, Court-Imposed Consequences - Parties

rg_summary: In Pletcher v. Vill. of Libertyville Police Pension Bd., No. 2-24-0416, 2025 Ill. App. Unpub. LEXIS 2115 (Nov. 24, 2025), the court granted defendant’s motion for sanctions against the pro se plaintiff pursuant to Illinois Supreme Court Rule 375(a). Specifically, the plaintiff filed a complaint for an administrative review of the Village of Libertyville Pension Board’s decision on plaintiff’s application for disability pension and a supporting brief. The court found that the plaintiff’s brief cited to multiple cases that do not exist, and also cited to “actual cases” but with “fictitious quotes and holdings.” The court concluded that these fictitious citations were “hallucinated by [GenAI]” and that the plaintiff violated Rule 341(h)(7). As a result, the court sanctioned the plaintiff by striking plaintiff’s brief and dismissing his appeal.

rg_summary: In Mills v. Rocket Mortg. LLC, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 231080 (W.D. La. Nov. 24, 2025), the court adopted the magistrate judge’s report and recommendation, and warned the plaintiffs of sanctions, including “the striking of their briefs, fines, or other appropriate actions” if they misuse GenAI tools in future filings. In doing so, Judge Doughty referred to the preceding report and recommendation issued by Magistrate Judge Kayla Dye McClusky that highlighted the plaintiffs’ presumed use of GenAI given their citations to nonexistent cases, inaccurate citations, and “mischaracterizations of cases which do exist.” See Mills v. Rocket Mortg. LLC, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 232163 (W.D. La. Nov. 6, 2025).

rg_summary: In Mangasarian v. City of Glendale, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 270943 (C.D. Cal. Nov. 21, 2025), the court suspected that the pro se plaintiffs “prepared certain filings with the assistance of a [GenAI] service.” The court warned the parties that any filings prepared using GenAI must include a declaration disclosing the GenAI use and certifying that the filer independently verified the accuracy of the contents as in compliance with Fed. R. Civ. P. 11. Judge Scarsi further warned that failure to follow this directive may result in sanctions. The court denied the plaintiffs’ application for a temporary restraining order on other grounds.

rg_summary: In Gupta v. Safko et al., No. CV 2024-1296-SEM, 2025 WL 3269046, at *1 (Del. Ch. Nov. 21, 2025), the court denied defendants’ request that pro plaintiff should be sanctioned. Although the court concluded that the plaintiff’s filings “do appear to have been generated through GenAI,” because of his pro se status and the fact that there was no “clear showing” of hallucinations or “glaring” misrepresentations, the court ultimately declined to impose sanctions. Senior Magistrate Molina did, however, order that the plaintiff must not only “ensure every future filing” is accurate, but also “certify any future use of GenAI” to the court by identifying what GenAI tool was used and how, and that it was reviewed for accuracy.
rg_tags: Generative AI Usage, Requires Disclosure and/or Verification, Applies to AI Used for Filings/Drafting, Court-Imposed Consequences - Parties

rg_summary: In Anuj Gupta v. Safko, 2025 Del. Ch. LEXIS 2041 (Del. Ch. Nov. 21, 2025), the defendants requested sanctions under Court of Chancery Rule 11, as the pro se plaintiff seemingly generated his filings with a GenAI tool. Judge Molina indicated that this conduct “raises several concerns about the Plaintiff’s ability to satisfy Rule 11’s dictates,” wastes parties’ and the court’s resources, and poses “reputational harms to the legal system.” However, given the plaintiff’s pro se status and lack of “any clear showing of hallucinations or glaring legal or factual misrepresentations in his filings,” the court declined to impose sanctions. Nevertheless, the court did order the plaintiff to certify any future use of GenAI and warned that “this leniency will not continue” should the plaintiff fail to comply with the court’s certification requirement.
